citations.title: Promising animal models for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis drug discovery: a comprehensive update
citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of motor neurons. Several animal models have been generated to understand ALS pathogenesis. They have provided valuable insight into disease mechanisms and the development of therapeutic strategies. AREAS COVERED: In this review, the authors provide a concise overview of simple genetic model organisms, including C. elegans, Drosophila, zebrafish, and mouse genetic models t
citations.support_rationale: The abstract confirms that animal models provide insight into disease mechanisms and therapeutic strategies, which aligns with the claim's premise. However, it does not explicitly list the specific pathological processes (excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, etc.) mentioned in the claim.

citations.title: Swimming against ALS: How to model disease in zebrafish for pathophysiological and behavioral studies
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease that leads to progressive disability and motor impairment. Existing therapies provide modest improvements in patient survival, raising a need for new treatments for ALS. Zebrafish is a promising model animal for translational and fundamental research in ALS - it is an experimentally tractable vertebrate, with high homology to humans and an ample experimental toolbox. These advantages allow high-throughput study of behavioral and
citations.support_rationale: This reference supports the use of zebrafish models for studying pathophysiological phenotypes in ALS. citations.title: Biomagnification of cyanobacterial neurotoxins and neurodegenerative disease among the Chamorro people of Guam
citations.abstract: We here report biomagnification (the increasing accumulation of bioactive, often deleterious molecules through higher trophic levels of a food chain) of the neurotoxic nonprotein amino acid beta-methylamino-l-alanine (BMAA) in the Guam ecosystem. Free-living cyanobacteria produce 0.3 microg/g BMAA, but produce 2-37 microg/g as symbionts in the coralloid roots of cycad trees. BMAA is concentrated in the developing reproductive tissues of the cycad Cycas micronesica, averaging 9 microg/g in the fl
citations.support_rationale: This paper explicitly reports the biomagnification of BMAA in the Guam ecosystem and documents significantly elevated BMAA levels in the brains of Chamorro individuals who died of ALS-PDC. It directly links the high incidence of the disease in this population to the consumption of BMAA-contaminated flying foxes, supporting the claim of a correlation between exposure and disease.

ALS is poorly understood, although multiple studies have been proposed to explain the pathophysiological mechanisms of the disorder. citations.support_rationale: This review discusses transcriptomics in ALS generally and does not mention Edaravone, BMAA, or zebrafish models.

citations.title: Genomic and transcriptomic advances in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disorder and the most common motor neuron disease. ALS shows substantial clinical and molecular heterogeneity. In vitro and in vivo models coupled with multiomic techniques have provided important contributions to unraveling the pathomechanisms underlying ALS. citations.support_rationale: This review covers genomic and transcriptomic advances in ALS but lacks any specific data on Edaravone treatment in BMAA-induced zebrafish models.

citations.title: Transcriptomic Analysis of Zebrafish TDP-43 Transgenic Lines
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a late-onset progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects both upper and lower motor neurons, leading to muscle atrophy with spasticity and eventual death in 3 to 5 years after the disease onset. More than 50 mutations linked to ALS have been found in the gene TARDBP, encoding the protein TDP-43 that is the predominant component of neuronal inclusions in ALS. citations.support_rationale: While this study uses zebrafish to model ALS via TDP-43, it does not involve BMAA induction or test Edaravone as a rescue therapy.

citations.support_rationale: This article investigates cannabinoids in SOD1 mice, not Edaravone in BMAA-induced zebrafish larvae.

citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by progressive motor neuron loss, paralysis and death within 2-5 years of diagnosis. citations.title: A Simple Guideline to Assess the Characteristics of RNA-Seq Data
citations.abstract: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) techniques have been used to generate various molecular maps including genomes, epigenomes, and transcriptomes. Transcriptomes from a given cell population can be profiled via RNA-seq. However, there is no simple way to assess the characteristics of RNA-seq data systematically. citations.title: A perspective on therapies for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: can disease progression be curbed?
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease involving both upper and lower motor neurons, leading to paralysis and eventually death. Symptomatic treatments such as inhibition of salivation, alleviation of muscle cramps, and relief of spasticity and pain still play an important role in enhancing the quality of life. To date, riluzole and edaravone are the only two drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of ALS in a few countries. Whi
citations.support_rationale: The abstract discusses general ALS therapies and mentions edaravone only in the context of FDA approval and general efficacy questions, without providing any data on gene expression counts or comparisons between BMAA, CNR-401, or cannflavin A.

citations.title: URG4 mediates cell proliferation and cell cycle in osteosarcoma via GSK3β/β-catenin/cyclin D1 signaling pathway
citations.abstract: BACKGROUND: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common malignant bone tumors with the annual global incidence of approximately four per million. Upregulated gene 4 (URG4) expression in the osteosarcoma tissue is closely associated with recurrence, metastasis, and poor prognosis of osteosarcoma. However, the biological function and underlying mechanisms of URG4 in osteosarcoma have not been elucidated. This study aimed to explore the expression and underlying mechanism of URG4 in osteosarcoma. METHOD
citations.support_rationale: The reference discusses URG4, not URGCP, and while it confirms URG4 drives proliferation via the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, it does not support the specific claim that URGCP was reduced.

citations.title: Astrocytic and microglial cells as the modulators of neuroinflammation in Alzheimer’s disease
citations.abstract: Neuroinflammation is instigated by the misfiring of immune cells in the central nervous system (CNS) involving microglia and astrocytes as key cell-types. Neuroinflammation is a consequence of CNS injury, infection, toxicity, or autoimmunity. It is favorable as well as a detrimental process for neurodevelopment and associated processes. Transient activation of inflammatory response involving release of cytokines and growth factors positively affects the development and post-injury tissue. Howeve
citations.support_rationale: This review focuses on neuroinflammation, astrocytes, and microglia in Alzheimer's disease, containing no information regarding URGCP, Wnt signaling, or cell proliferation mechanisms.

Aberrant excitability of motoneurons has been implicated in the pathogenesis of selective motoneuronal death in ALS. D-serine, an endogenous coagonist of N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors, exacerbates motoneuronal death and is increased both in patients with sporadic/familial ALS and in a G93A-SOD1 mouse model of ALS (mSOD1 mouse). citations.support_rationale: This reference discusses D-amino acid oxidase and D-serine in the context of ALS and motoneuron degeneration, with no mention of CNR-401, gene ontology enrichment, or extracellular matrix (ECM) processes.

citations.title: Focus on the Role of D-serine and D-amino Acid Oxidase in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis/Motor Neuron Disease (ALS)
citations.abstract: We have investigated a pathogenic mutation in D-amino acid oxidase (DAO), DAOR199W, associated with familial Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) that impairs D-serine metabolism and causes protein aggregation, autophagy and cell death in motor neuron cell lines. citations.title: The extracellular matrix as modifier of neuroinflammation and remyelination in multiple sclerosis
citations.abstract: Remyelination failure contributes to axonal loss and progression of disability in multiple sclerosis. citations.title: Mapping RNA‐seq Reads with STAR
citations.abstract: Mapping of large sets of high-throughput sequencing reads to a reference genome is one of the foundational steps in RNA-seq data analysis. The STAR software package performs this task with high levels of accuracy and speed. In addition to detecting annotated and novel splice junctions, STAR is capable of discovering more complex RNA sequence arrangements, such as chimeric and circular RNA. citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of motor neurons. Several animal models have been generated to understand ALS pathogenesis. They have provided valuable insight into disease mechanisms and the development of therapeutic strategies. citations.support_rationale: The abstract confirms that animal models provide insight into disease mechanisms and therapeutic strategies, which aligns with the claim's premise. Existing therapies provide modest improvements in patient survival, raising a need for new treatments for ALS. Zebrafish is a promising model animal for translational and fundamental research in ALS - it is an experimentally tractable vertebrate, with high homology to humans and an ample experimental toolbox. citations.title: Biomagnification of cyanobacterial neurotoxins and neurodegenerative disease among the Chamorro people of Guam
citations.abstract: We here report biomagnification (the increasing accumulation of bioactive, often deleterious molecules through higher trophic levels of a food chain) of the neurotoxic nonprotein amino acid beta-methylamino-l-alanine (BMAA) in the Guam ecosystem. Free-living cyanobacteria produce 0.3 microg/g BMAA, but produce 2-37 microg/g as symbionts in the coralloid roots of cycad trees. BMAA is concentrated in the developing reproductive tissues of the cycad Cycas micronesica, averaging 9 microg/g in the fl
citations.support_rationale: This paper explicitly reports the biomagnification of BMAA in the Guam ecosystem and documents significantly elevated BMAA levels in the brains of Chamorro individuals who died of ALS-PDC. It directly links the high incidence of the disease in this population to the consumption of BMAA-contaminated flying foxes, supporting the claim of a correlation between exposure and disease.

ALS is poorly understood, although multiple studies have been proposed to explain the pathophysiological mechanisms of the disorder. citations.support_rationale: This review discusses transcriptomics in ALS generally and does not mention Edaravone, BMAA, or zebrafish models.

citations.title: Genomic and transcriptomic advances in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disorder and the most common motor neuron disease. ALS shows substantial clinical and molecular heterogeneity. citations.support_rationale: This article investigates cannabinoids in SOD1 mice, not Edaravone in BMAA-induced zebrafish larvae.

citations.title: A perspective on therapies for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: can disease progression be curbed?
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease involving both upper and lower motor neurons, leading to paralysis and eventually death. Symptomatic treatments such as inhibition of salivation, alleviation of muscle cramps, and relief of spasticity and pain still play an important role in enhancing the quality of life. To date, riluzole and edaravone are the only two drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of ALS in a few countries. Whi

citations.title: URG4 mediates cell proliferation and cell cycle in osteosarcoma via GSK3β/β-catenin/cyclin D1 signaling pathway
citations.abstract: BACKGROUND: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common malignant bone tumors with the annual global incidence of approximately four per million. Upregulated gene 4 (URG4) expression in the osteosarcoma tissue is closely associated with recurrence, metastasis, and poor prognosis of osteosarcoma. However, the biological function and underlying mechanisms of URG4 in osteosarcoma have not been elucidated. This study aimed to explore the expression and underlying mechanism of URG4 in osteosarcoma. METHOD
citations.support_rationale: The reference discusses URG4, not URGCP, and while it confirms URG4 drives proliferation via the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, it does not support the specific claim that URGCP was reduced.

citations.title: Astrocytic and microglial cells as the modulators of neuroinflammation in Alzheimer’s disease
citations.abstract: Neuroinflammation is instigated by the misfiring of immune cells in the central nervous system (CNS) involving microglia and astrocytes as key cell-types. Neuroinflammation is a consequence of CNS injury, infection, toxicity, or autoimmunity. It is favorable as well as a detrimental process for neurodevelopment and associated processes. Transient activation of inflammatory response involving release of cytokines and growth factors positively affects the development and post-injury tissue. Howeve
citations.support_rationale: This review focuses on neuroinflammation, astrocytes, and microglia in Alzheimer's disease, containing no information regarding URGCP, Wnt signaling, or cell proliferation mechanisms.

citations.abstract: Spinal cord injury (SCI) denotes damage to both the structure and function of the spinal cord, primarily manifesting as sensory and motor deficits caused by disruptions in neural transmission pathways, potentially culminating in irreversible paralysis. citations.support_rationale: This reference discusses D-amino acid oxidase and D-serine in the context of ALS and motoneuron degeneration, with no mention of CNR-401, gene ontology enrichment, or extracellular matrix (ECM) processes.

citations.abstract: Drug bioavailability is a crucial aspect of pharmacology, affecting the effectiveness of drug therapy. Understanding how drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and eliminated in patients' bodies is essential to ensure proper and safe treatment. This publication aims to highlight the relevance of drug bioavailability research and its importance in therapy. In addition to biochemical activity, bioavailability also plays a critical role in achieving the desired therapeutic effects. This may
citations.support_rationale: The reference discusses general concepts of drug bioavailability and pharmacology, containing no mention of enrichment analysis, inflammatory pathways, or anti-inflammatory activity.

citations.title: Promising animal models for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis drug discovery: a comprehensive update
citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of motor neurons. Several animal models have been generated to understand ALS pathogenesis. They have provided valuable insight into disease mechanisms and the development of therapeutic strategies. citations.support_rationale: This review focuses on genetic models and general therapeutic strategies for ALS but does not mention BMAA or specific toxin-induced phenotypes like oxidative stress or neuromuscular junction disruption.

citations.title: Swimming against ALS: How to model disease in zebrafish for pathophysiological and behavioral studies
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease that leads to progressive disability and motor impairment. Existing therapies provide modest improvements in patient survival, raising a need for new treatments for ALS. Zebrafish is a promising model animal for translational and fundamental research in ALS - it is an experimentally tractable vertebrate, with high homology to humans and an ample experimental toolbox. citations.support_rationale: While this review discusses zebrafish models for ALS and mentions toxin combination studies generally, it does not provide specific evidence that BMAA induces the claimed phenotypes.

citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of motor neurons. Several animal models have been generated to understand ALS pathogenesis. They have provided valuable insight into disease mechanisms and the development of therapeutic strategies. AREAS COVERED: In this review, the authors provide a concise overview of simple genetic model organisms, including C. elegans, Drosophila, zebrafish, and mouse genetic models t
citations.support_rationale: The abstract confirms that animal models provide insight into disease mechanisms and therapeutic strategies, which aligns with the claim's premise. However, it does not explicitly list the specific pathological processes (excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, etc.) mentioned in the claim.

citations.abstract: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease that leads to progressive disability and motor impairment. Existing therapies provide modest improvements in patient survival, raising a need for new treatments for ALS. Zebrafish is a promising model animal for translational and fundamental research in ALS - it is an experimentally tractable vertebrate, with high homology to humans and an ample experimental toolbox. citations.support_rationale: This reference supports the use of zebrafish models for studying pathophysiological phenotypes in ALS. citations.title: Biomagnification of cyanobacterial neurotoxins and neurodegenerative disease among the Chamorro people of Guam
citations.abstract: We here report biomagnification (the increasing accumulation of bioactive, often deleterious molecules through higher trophic levels of a food chain) of the neurotoxic nonprotein amino acid beta-methylamino-l-alanine (BMAA) in the Guam ecosystem. Free-living cyanobacteria produce 0.3 microg/g BMAA, but produce 2-37 microg/g as symbionts in the coralloid roots of cycad trees. BMAA is concentrated in the developing reproductive tissues of the cycad Cycas micronesica, averaging 9 microg/g in the fl
citations.support_rationale: This paper explicitly reports the biomagnification of BMAA in the Guam ecosystem and documents significantly elevated BMAA levels in the brains of Chamorro individuals who died of ALS-PDC. It directly links the high incidence of the disease in this population to the consumption of BMAA-contaminated flying foxes, supporting the claim of a correlation between exposure and disease.

ALS is poorly understood, although multiple studies have been proposed to explain the pathophysiological mechanisms of the disorder. citations.support_rationale: This review discusses transcriptomics in ALS generally but does not mention CNR-401, BMAA, or any specific therapeutic interventions in zebrafish models.

citations.title: Genomic and transcriptomic advances in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disorder and the most common motor neuron disease. ALS shows substantial clinical and molecular heterogeneity. In vitro and in vivo models coupled with multiomic techniques have provided important contributions to unraveling the pathomechanisms underlying ALS. citations.support_rationale: This review covers genomic and transcriptomic advances in ALS but lacks any specific data regarding CNR-401, BMAA toxicity, or zebrafish-based motor neuron degeneration studies.

More than 50 mutations linked to ALS have been found in the gene TARDBP, encoding the protein TDP-43 that is the predominant component of neuronal inclusions in ALS. citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by progressive motor neuron loss, paralysis and death within 2-5 years of diagnosis. Currently, no effective pharmacological agents exist for the treatment of this devastating disease. Neuroinflammation may accelerate the progression of ALS. citations.support_rationale: This study demonstrates CB2 agonist efficacy in SOD1 mice, yet it fails to address CNR-401, BMAA, or zebrafish models, thus not supporting the specific claim.

citations.title: Functional characterization of the cannabinoid receptors 1 and 2 in zebrafish larvae using behavioral analysis
citations.abstract: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) comprises the cannabinoids anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol and the cannabinoid receptors 1 and 2 (Cnr1 and Cnr2). The function of these receptors in relation to zebrafish larval behavior is poorly understood, even though the zebrafish larva has become a versatile animal model in biomedical research. The objective of the present study is to characterize the function of Cnr1 and Cnr2 in relation to behavior in zebrafish. Behavioral analysis of zebrafish larva
citations.support_rationale: This study establishes that cannabinoid receptors (Cnr1/Cnr2) modulate locomotor activity in zebrafish, providing a mechanistic basis for using zebrafish to assess motor function, but it does not test CNR-401 against BMAA.

citations.title: A perspective on therapies for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: can disease progression be curbed?
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease involving both upper and lower motor neurons, leading to paralysis and eventually death. Symptomatic treatments such as inhibition of salivation, alleviation of muscle cramps, and relief of spasticity and pain still play an important role in enhancing the quality of life. To date, riluzole and edaravone are the only two drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of ALS in a few countries. Whi

citations.title: URG4 mediates cell proliferation and cell cycle in osteosarcoma via GSK3β/β-catenin/cyclin D1 signaling pathway
citations.abstract: BACKGROUND: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common malignant bone tumors with the annual global incidence of approximately four per million. Upregulated gene 4 (URG4) expression in the osteosarcoma tissue is closely associated with recurrence, metastasis, and poor prognosis of osteosarcoma. However, the biological function and underlying mechanisms of URG4 in osteosarcoma have not been elucidated. This study aimed to explore the expression and underlying mechanism of URG4 in osteosarcoma. METHOD
citations.support_rationale: This study focuses on URG4 in osteosarcoma (bone cancer) and its role in cell proliferation via GSK3β/β-catenin signaling, lacking any mention of glial cells, neuroinflammation, or the CNS context required to support the claim.

citations.title: Astrocytic and microglial cells as the modulators of neuroinflammation in Alzheimer’s disease
citations.abstract: Neuroinflammation is instigated by the misfiring of immune cells in the central nervous system (CNS) involving microglia and astrocytes as key cell-types. Neuroinflammation is a consequence of CNS injury, infection, toxicity, or autoimmunity. It is favorable as well as a detrimental process for neurodevelopment and associated processes. Transient activation of inflammatory response involving release of cytokines and growth factors positively affects the development and post-injury tissue. Howeve
citations.support_rationale: The abstract confirms that glial cells (microglia and astrocytes) drive neuroinflammation in the CNS, supporting the first part of the claim regarding glial proliferation and inflammation, but it does not provide evidence regarding URGCP/Wnt signaling or its dampening effects.

Notably, the pronounced upregulation of the Wnt signaling pathway post-SCI holds promise for neural regeneration and repair. Activat

citations.title: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis as a protein level, non-genomic disease: Therapy with S2RM exosome released molecules
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a rapidly progressing neurodegenerative disease that leads to death. No effective treatments are currently available. citations.title: Biomagnification of cyanobacterial neurotoxins and neurodegenerative disease among the Chamorro people of Guam
citations.abstract: We here report biomagnification (the increasing accumulation of bioactive, often deleterious molecules through higher trophic levels of a food chain) of the neurotoxic nonprotein amino acid beta-methylamino-l-alanine (BMAA) in the Guam ecosystem. Free-living cyanobacteria produce 0.3 microg/g BMAA, but produce 2-37 microg/g as symbionts in the coralloid roots of cycad trees. BMAA is concentrated in the developing reproductive tissues of the cycad Cycas micronesica, averaging 9 microg/g in the fl
citations.support_rationale: This paper explicitly reports the biomagnification of BMAA in the Guam ecosystem and documents significantly elevated BMAA levels in the brains of Chamorro individuals who died of ALS-PDC. It directly links the high incidence of the disease in this population to the consumption of BMAA-contaminated flying foxes, supporting the claim of a correlation between exposure and disease.

citations.title: A perspective on therapies for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: can disease progression be curbed?
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease involving both upper and lower motor neurons, leading to paralysis and eventually death. Symptomatic treatments such as inhibition of salivation, alleviation of muscle cramps, and relief of spasticity and pain still play an important role in enhancing the quality of life. To date, riluzole and edaravone are the only two drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of ALS in a few countries. Whi

citations.title: URG4 mediates cell proliferation and cell cycle in osteosarcoma via GSK3β/β-catenin/cyclin D1 signaling pathway
citations.abstract: BACKGROUND: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common malignant bone tumors with the annual global incidence of approximately four per million. Upregulated gene 4 (URG4) expression in the osteosarcoma tissue is closely associated with recurrence, metastasis, and poor prognosis of osteosarcoma. However, the biological function and underlying mechanisms of URG4 in osteosarcoma have not been elucidated. This study aimed to explore the expression and underlying mechanism of URG4 in osteosarcoma. METHOD
citations.support_rationale: This study focuses on URG4 in osteosarcoma (bone cancer) and its role in cell proliferation via GSK3β/β-catenin signaling, lacking any mention of glial cells, neuroinflammation, or the CNS context required to support the claim.

Still, its specific role in neurodegenerative diseases, such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), remains studied. This research investigates the role of SHH and PI3K/AKT signaling pathway proteins on ALS development in a SOD1-G93A transgenic mouse model. After injection of SHH and PI3K/AKT signaling pathway inhibitors or agonists in hSOD1-G93A (9 weeks of age) transgenic mi
citations.support_rationale: This study investigates the Sonic Hedgehog pathway's mediation of PI3K/Akt in an ALS model, which is mechanistically distinct from the specific claim about CNR401 mitigating BMAA-induced oxidative stress.

citations.abstract: Neuron injury is closely associated with the development of various diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, spinal cord injury, ischemic stroke, and depression. The phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)/protein kinase B(Akt) signaling pathway is vital for neuron cell growth, development, and survival and can suppress cell apoptosis via downregulating proapoptosis proteins. In recent years, a growing body of research has suggested that natural agents are beneficial for preventing or treating neuron inj

citations.abstract: BACKGROUND: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common malignant bone tumors with the annual global incidence of approximately four per million. Upregulated gene 4 (URG4) expression in the osteosarcoma tissue is closely associated with recurrence, metastasis, and poor prognosis of osteosarcoma. This study aimed to explore the expression and underlying mechanism of URG4 in osteosarcoma. METHOD

citations.title: Uracil‐DNA glycosylases—Structural and functional perspectives on an essential family of DNA repair enzymes
citations.abstract: Uracil-DNA glycosylases (UDGs) are evolutionarily conserved DNA repair enzymes that initiate the base excision repair pathway and remove uracil from DNA. The UDG superfamily is classified into six families based on their substrate specificity. This review focuses on the family I enzymes since these are the most extensively studied members of the superfamily. The structural basis for substrate specificity and base recognition as well as for DNA binding, nucleotide flipping and catalytic mechanism
citations.support_rationale: This review establishes the fundamental role of Uracil-DNA glycosylases in DNA repair, providing background on the repair machinery, but it does not address BMAA toxicity or motor neuron expression data.

citations.title: A perspective on therapies for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: can disease progression be curbed?
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease involving both upper and lower motor neurons, leading to paralysis and eventually death. Symptomatic treatments such as inhibition of salivation, alleviation of muscle cramps, and relief of spasticity and pain still play an important role in enhancing the quality of life. To date, riluzole and edaravone are the only two drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of ALS in a few countries. Whi
citations.support_rationale: The abstract explicitly states that riluzole has 'adequate consensus on the modest efficacy' and notes that edaravone's efficacy in slowing progression remains an open question, directly validating the claim that current therapies provide only modest benefits. It further concludes that 'identification of novel therapeutic strategies is urgently needed,' which aligns with the claim's assertion of an urgent need for new approaches.

aspirational.evidence_summary: The cited review directly confirms the modest efficacy of approved ALS drugs (riluzole and edaravone) and explicitly calls for the urgent development of novel therapeutic strategies, fully supporting the claim.
aspirational.claim_narrative: 5- Transcriptomic Analysis of a Cannabis-Derived Neuroprotective Therapy in a Zebrafish Model of ALS presents the claim 'Current therapies, such as riluzole and edaravone, provide only modest benefits, underscoring the urgent need for new therapeutic approaches that address the complex molecular mechanisms underlying ALS pathogenesis.' in the INTRODUCTION section. This claim was deemed supported (The Introduction explicitly states that current therapies like riluzole and edaravone offer only modest benefits, highlighting the need for new approaches targeting complex ALS mechanisms.). Evidence grade: strong — The cited review directly confirms the modest efficacy of approved ALS drugs (riluzole and edaravone) and explicitly calls for the urgent development of novel therapeutic strategies, fully supporting the claim. This claim is rated as extremely relevant for the core of this research.

citations.title: Promising animal models for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis drug discovery: a comprehensive update
citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of motor neurons. Several animal models have been generated to understand ALS pathogenesis. They have provided valuable insight into disease mechanisms and the development of therapeutic strategies. AREAS COVERED: In this review, the authors provide a concise overview of simple genetic model organisms, including C. elegans, Drosophila, zebrafish, and mouse genetic models t
citations.support_rationale: The abstract explicitly states that animal models have provided 'valuable insight' and that progress is being enabled by 'promising animal models,' which contradicts the claim that they 'often fail to capture the full spectrum.' While it mentions challenges in translation, it does not support the assertion that the models themselves fail to capture disease pathology.

citations.title: Swimming against ALS: How to model disease in zebrafish for pathophysiological and behavioral studies
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease that leads to progressive disability and motor impairment. Existing therapies provide modest improvements in patient survival, raising a need for new treatments for ALS. Zebrafish is a promising model animal for translational and fundamental research in ALS - it is an experimentally tractable vertebrate, with high homology to humans and an ample experimental toolbox. These advantages allow high-throughput study of behavioral and
citations.support_rationale: This abstract describes zebrafish as a 'promising model' with 'high homology to humans' and discusses the 'validity' of models, but it does not provide evidence that genetic models fail to capture the full spectrum of patient pathology.

citations.title: Biomagnification of cyanobacterial neurotoxins and neurodegenerative disease among the Chamorro people of Guam
citations.abstract: We here report biomagnification (the increasing accumulation of bioactive, often deleterious molecules through higher trophic levels of a food chain) of the neurotoxic nonprotein amino acid beta-methylamino-l-alanine (BMAA) in the Guam ecosystem. Free-living cyanobacteria produce 0.3 microg/g BMAA, but produce 2-37 microg/g as symbionts in the coralloid roots of cycad trees. BMAA is concentrated in the developing reproductive tissues of the cycad Cycas micronesica, averaging 9 microg/g in the fl
citations.support_rationale: This reference discusses the biomagnification of BMAA in the Guam ecosystem and human populations, but it does not mention zebrafish models, motor neuron dysfunction, or the evaluation of candidate therapeutics.

ALS is poorly understood, although multiple studies have been proposed to explain the pathophysiological mechanisms of the disorder. citations.support_rationale: This review discusses transcriptomics in ALS but does not mention cannabinoid-flavonoid formulations or their combination with transcriptomic profiling.

citations.title: Genomic and transcriptomic advances in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disorder and the most common motor neuron disease. ALS shows substantial clinical and molecular heterogeneity. In vitro and in vivo models coupled with multiomic techniques have provided important contributions to unraveling the pathomechanisms underlying ALS. To date, despite promising results and accumulating knowledge, an effective treatment is still lacking. Here, we provide an overview of the literature on the use of genomics, epige
citations.support_rationale: While this review covers transcriptomics and multi-omic approaches in ALS, it lacks any mention of cannabinoid-flavonoid formulations.

citations.support_rationale: This study uses RNA sequencing in zebrafish to analyze TDP-43 mutations but does not involve cannabinoid-flavonoid formulations.

citations.support_rationale: This article investigates the effects of synthetic cannabinoids on SOD1 mice but does not include transcriptomic profiling or flavonoids.

citations.title: The CB2 cannabinoid agonist AM‐1241 prolongs survival in a transgenic mouse model of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis when initiated at symptom onset
citations.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by progressive motor neuron loss, paralysis and death within 2-5 years of diagnosis. citations.support_rationale: This study focuses on CB2 agonists and survival in ALS mice without mentioning transcriptomic profiling or flavonoid components.

citations.title: STAR: ultrafast universal RNA-seq aligner
citations.abstract: MOTIVATION: Accurate alignment of high-throughput RNA-seq data is a challenging and yet unsolved problem because of the non-contiguous transcript structure, relatively short read lengths and constantly increasing throughput of the sequencing technologies. Currently available RNA-seq aligners suffer from high mapping error rates, low mapping speed, read length limitation and mapping biases. RESULTS: To align our large (>80 billon reads) ENCODE Transcriptome RNA-seq dataset, we developed the Splic
citations.support_rationale: This reference describes the STAR aligner for mapping RNA-seq reads, which is a data processing step, but it does not discuss normalization methods, automated pathway curation, or the specific pipeline architecture for ALS therapeutic target identification.

citations.title: Mapping RNA‐seq Reads with STAR
citations.abstract: Mapping of large sets of high-throughput sequencing reads to a reference genome is one of the foundational steps in RNA-seq data analysis. The STAR software package performs this task with high levels of accuracy and speed. In addition to detecting annotated and novel splice junctions, STAR is capable of discovering more complex RNA sequence arrangements, such as chimeric and circular RNA. STAR can align spliced sequences of any length with moderate error rates, providing scalability for emergin
citations.support_rationale: Similar to reference 24, this review focuses on the utility and protocols for using the STAR aligner for general RNA-seq analysis, offering no evidence regarding the specific normalization, curation, or reproducibility features of the claimed ALS pipeline.

citations.title: STAR: ultrafast universal RNA-seq aligner
citations.abstract: MOTIVATION: Accurate alignment of high-throughput RNA-seq data is a challenging and yet unsolved problem because of the non-contiguous transcript structure, relatively short read lengths and constantly increasing throughput of the sequencing technologies. Currently available RNA-seq aligners suffer from high mapping error rates, low mapping speed, read length limitation and mapping biases. RESULTS: To align our large (>80 billon reads) ENCODE Transcriptome RNA-seq dataset, we developed the Splic
citations.support_rationale: This reference describes the STAR aligner for mapping RNA-seq reads, which is a data processing step, but it does not discuss normalization methods, automated pathway curation, or the specific pipeline architecture for ALS therapeutic target identification.

citations.title: Mapping RNA‐seq Reads with STAR
citations.abstract: Mapping of large sets of high-throughput sequencing reads to a reference genome is one of the foundational steps in RNA-seq data analysis. The STAR software package performs this task with high levels of accuracy and speed. In addition to detecting annotated and novel splice junctions, STAR is capable of discovering more complex RNA sequence arrangements, such as chimeric and circular RNA. STAR can align spliced sequences of any length with moderate error rates, providing scalability for emergin
citations.support_rationale: Similar to reference 24, this review focuses on the utility and protocols for using the STAR aligner for general RNA-seq analysis, offering no evidence regarding the specific normalization, curation, or reproducibility features of the claimed ALS pipeline.
